sop.coo_evaluation:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaints & incident escalation** – no documented process for handling injuries, billing disputes, harassment, or facility safety issues; critical for liability & retention
- **Financial close & reporting** – no SOP for monthly/quarterly reconciliation, churn analysis, or P&L review (assumed buried in General)
- **Coach performance management & underperformance** – no formal review cadence, coaching feedback loops, or removal procedures
- **Member win-back / lapsed reactivation** – no documented outreach campaign or criteria for who/when to target
- **Duty of care & health screening** – no SOP for medical questionnaires, contraindications, or when to refer members to professionals
- **New member onboarding experience** – likely exists but probably fragmented (induction, goal-setting, buddy system unclear)
- **Class cancellation & substitute protocols** – no clear SOP when a coach is sick or no-shows; member comms timing
- **Data retention & GDPR compliance** – no documented deletion/archival schedule for member data, contracts, or incident files

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Coach SOPs (17/17)** – likely missing: conflict resolution between coaches, social media conduct, appearance standards, off-hours client interaction boundaries
- **Client SOPs (22/22)** – check if these cover: contract renewal/non-renewal decisions, member tier upgrades/downgrades, platinum/VIP paths; likely thin on retention mechanics
- **Marketing (10/12)** – Email Newsletter & Blog Post are missing; also likely missing: retargeting lapsed members, referral incentive mechanics, seasonal campaign templates
- **General (3/3)** – almost certainly incomplete; review: crisis comms (injury, closure), staffing contingency, pricing/discount approval authority
- **Facilities (4/

kpi.advice:
# The Forge — Fractional CFO Action Plan

## INCREASE REVENUE

- **Pricing review overdue.** AR/M (average revenue per member) has collapsed from £170.43 (December) to £120.48 (June) — a 29% drop despite member count growing from 124 to 147. You've either discounted heavily post-relocation or onboarded price-sensitive cohorts. Audit your current pricing tier mix; increase base membership by £2–5/month and push higher-margin class packs or PT bundles to new 147-member base. Target: restore AR/M to £155+ by September.
- **Churn control critical.** June showed 0% attrition (good), but May was 5.74% and April was 8.33% — the relocation disrupted retention. Implement 30-day post-move check-ins and a referral incentive (£20 credit per referral) to lock in the 147 members and grow to 165+ by Q3.
- **Upsell aggressively to the bigger space.** Your studio is now 2x capacity but revenue June (£17.7k) barely exceeds May (£20.2k) — you've *lost* £2.5k month-on-month despite gaining 25 members. Premium class slots (peak hours), small-group training, and locker/towel add-ons should be pushed hard. Target: 5% of members on a paid add-on by August.

## REDUCE EXPENSES

- **Non-people costs are the crisis.** June non-people expenses £9,390 are **2x the September–November average (£4.5k)**. Even excluding April's relocation fit-out, Jan–June averages £9.3k — unsustainable against £17.7k revenue. Audit utilities, rent, equipment maintenance, insurance, and supplies line-by-line. Identify £2–3k of overspend and negotiate supplier terms; rent on the bigger space may be higher but should not alone account for a 100% increase.
- **Staffing creep.** People costs rose from £2.5k (November peak efficiency) to £3.7k (June). You've added headcount for the bigger studio — necessary — but June's £3,686 against £17.7k revenue (20.8% of revenue) is above target. Benchmark: aim for 18% people-cost ratio. Review rota efficiency; shift to part-time or contractor model if full-time headcount exceeds needs.

## IMPROVE PROFITABILITY & TAX

staff.advice:
• **Redistribute Grace's classes immediately**: her Build: Lower (17), Build: Upper (9), Elevate (6) and Strongevity (3) must move to JoJo and Eilis. This is the priority — Grace should do zero delivery coaching by next month.

• **JoJo is carrying too much**: 133 sessions (44 PT + 20 scans + 69 classes) makes her a bottleneck. Shift 6–8 of her Circuits and Mobility classes to Eilis, and move 4–5 Build: Upper to Eilis to free JoJo for PT and higher-value work.

• **Eilis is under-used and ready**: she's at 48 sessions with good class variety (Elevate, Build, Plate, Capacity). She can safely absorb Grace's Build: Lower, some of JoJo's Circuits, and take on more PT (she currently has 11; capacity exists to grow toward 18–20).

• **Cover Grace's No Sweat Intro and PT**: assign her 3× No Sweat Intros to JoJo (1) and Eilis (2), and her 0 PT load is naturally gone. Her 9 Body Composition Scans are not real coaching — strip these out and any coach can do them in 5 minutes.

• **Stabilise the Tuesday constraint**: JoJo owns Tuesdays — confirm which specific Tuesday classes she must keep, then map Grace's Elevate and Strongevity to other days so Eilis can take them without collision.

• **Target for next month**: JoJo ~100–110 sessions (PT + core classes), Eilis ~65–75 sessions (classes + growing PT), Grace = 0 delivery.

class_stats.suggestion:
**For quiet studio time gaps:**

1. Add a paid 30-min "Open Gym + Coach" block on Thursday and Monday evenings (your quietest days) — members book a PT-style slot for unstructured lifting with coach feedback, filling dead time and converting open-gym interest into revenue.

2. Run a weekly "Mobility + Coffee" workshop Saturday mornings before your quietest day picks up — 45 mins of guided mobility work followed by informal coffee chat; low overhead, builds Saturday attendance habit, pairs well with Mobility class promotion.

**For quieter classes themselves:**

3. Move Engine (52 attendees) to Tuesday or Wednesday morning and rebrand it as a 20-min "Pre-Build warm-up" or "Lunchtime cardio burner" — give it a time-slot advantage on your busiest days and a function-focused identity, then offer a discounted "Build + Engine bundle" to Build class regulars to drive crossover.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Build — The Forge

**1. Audit first, then structure**
Pull your last 3 months of booking/enquiry data. What are members actually paying for? What do coaches keep selling? Build the menu around *what's working*, not what you think should exist. You'll spot the gaps (e.g., "people want 4-week body recomp packages but you're only selling monthly").

**2. Create two separate documents — don't try one menu that serves both**
*Coach version:* Pricing, margins, what they can upsell, commission structure, how to position each tier. *Member version:* Benefits-focused (not cost), what each tier unlocks, social proof ("X women per month choose this"). Your coaches sell differently than your website does.

**3. Price in tiers with a deliberate anchor**
Don't list every option. Offer 3 tiers max (Good/Better/Best). Make the middle tier 15-20% cheaper than "à la carte" pricing so it looks like the obvious choice. Test this: if nobody's buying your top tier after 2 weeks, it's priced wrong or positioned wrong — fix fast.

**4. Embed decision-friction removers**
Add a "starter package" (first session + 2-week trial rate) so new members don't have to commit to a full program immediately. Coaches hate this? Train them to see it as a *conversion tool*, not a discount. Track who converts from trial to full package.

**5. Live-test immediately, don't perfect first**
Send the draft to your 3-4 most vocal members and your top coach this week. Get feedback in 48 hours.

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement for The Forge

**Immediate actions:**

• **Mine your actual customers for FAQs** — Don't guess. Pull your last 50 enquiries/calls/emails. What questions do women ask *before* committing? ("Do I need to be fit already?" "What's the changing room situation?" "Is it intimidating?"). Build your landing copy around *those* specific objections, not what you think matters.

• **Lead with your differentiation in the first 8 seconds** — Women's-only gym isn't a feature, it's your moat. But spell out the *actual benefit* immediately: safety, no male gaze anxiety, community vibe, whatever resonates with YOUR ideal member. Test two versions: one emphasizing that benefit vs. one that doesn't. Measure which converts better.

• **Create a micro-funnel by membership type** — Don't make beginner and advanced lifters click through the same landing page. Segment: "Getting fit for the first time?" → one page. "Already train, looking for women's space?" → different page. Different objections, different copy.

• **Add a simple objection-killer section** — Format: "Common concern | The reality at The Forge." Visuals help (before/afters, member testimonials answering those real questions). Keep it to 3-4 objections max.

• **A/B test urgency/scarcity tactically** — Limited spots? Introductory rates ending? Test whether adding this (if true) moves the needle. Measure conversion rate before/after—don't assume it helps.

What's your current landing page conversion rate (visitor to trial/inquiry)?

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Internal Feedback System for The Forge

**Right now, today:**
- **Skip the form.** Use what you already have: Google Forms embedded on your app/website with ONE question rotating monthly ("What should we add?" / "Rate your experience" / "What class do you want?"). Takes 30 seconds to complete. Forms get ignored; you need friction-free capture.

**Quick implementation (this week):**
- **Attach feedback to natural touchpoints.** Train your front desk to verbally ask 2-3 members per shift: "What's working? What isn't?" Capture answers in a shared Slack channel. This works better than a form because people talk more freely, and *you hear the tone* — frustration vs. enthusiasm.

**Make it actually useful:**
- **Create a visible "We listened" loop.** Post monthly: "5 members asked for X. Here's what we're doing." (even if it's "we're testing it"). Members give more feedback when they see action. This is your competitive advantage vs. big gyms.

**Revenue angle:**
- **Mine upsell opportunities during check-ins.** When someone says "I'd love a nutrition program," you now have permission to pitch your nutrition coaching add-on. Feedback = qualified sales intel.

**Avoid this mistake:**
- **Don't make it anonymous.** You need names so you can follow up with advocates (expansion recruitment), spot churn risks early, and verify if feedback is from your power users or occasional visitors.

What's your current member communication channel? (App, email, WhatsApp group?)

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Plan for The Forge

**1. Reverse-engineer from your actual member journey first**
Don't start writing. Spend 2 hours this week shadowing/recording ONE member through each service type. What actually happens? What confuses them? What takes longest? This is your SOP skeleton—now you're documenting reality, not guessing.

**2. Create a single template that mirrors your sales process**
Each SOP follows this structure: (1) Pre-purchase expectations → (2) Day 1 onboarding steps → (3) Weekly/ongoing experience → (4) Escalation protocol. This isn't busywork—it's how members *actually* move through each service. Fuel Forward SOP looks different from Strongevity because the member journey is different.

**3. Assign one SOP per revenue stream owner, with 1-week deadlines**
Who's responsible for Fuel Forward success? That person writes that SOP. 500-word max. Video-record them walking through it (3 mins) instead of just text—members and staff learn faster. Due dates: Next 5 weeks, one SOP/week.

**4. Test SOPs with 3 members before finalizing**
Hand each SOP to a new member in that category. Watch them follow it without help. Where do they get stuck? Fix those gaps. A SOP that doesn't work with actual humans isn't an SOP—it's a filing exercise.

**5. Link SOPs directly to retention metrics you're tracking**
Once live, each SOP owns a specific metric (Strongevity = 90-day retention %, Fuel Forward = weekly engagement %). Review monthly. If a

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Content Calendar — Execution Plan for Grace

**Pick ONE platform first, own it, then expand**
Don't spread thin across Instagram, TikTok, and Facebook simultaneously. Start with Instagram (best for fitness community engagement). Post 3x/week there for 90 days, measure what sticks, *then* add a second platform. This prevents the "I created a calendar I can't maintain" trap.

**Use a stupidly simple batching system — one 90-min session per month**
Schedule a monthly "content shoot day" where Grace (or a staff member) films 12–15 short clips in one go: member transformations, class highlights, quick form tips, behind-the-scenes. Then drag them into a free tool like Buffer or Later, and schedule them across the month. Calendar done before month starts. No daily pressure.

**Make the calendar about YOUR competitive advantage, not generic fitness content**
"Women-only space" and "Belfast community" are your edges. Posts should showcase: member wins specific to women (strength gains, confidence shifts), local partnerships, what makes The Forge different from mixed gyms. Avoid copying big global fitness accounts—you'll lose that fight. Instead, become the go-to local authority.

**Tie calendar posts to revenue drivers**
Every 4th post should soft-promote (class sign-ups, referral program, membership tier upgrade). Not salesy—just "3 spots left in Thursday's power class." This turns followers into members, not just engagement vanity metrics.

**Automate the scheduling, not the thinking**
Use Buffer or Later (free versions work fine). But Grace still writes captions weekly (15 mins). Automation handles posting time, not creativity.

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Executing Videography Sessions with Jordan

• **Lock it in NOW with a standing booking** — Contact Jordan today and book 8-week slots 6 months ahead (not ad-hoc). This removes the "when should we do this?" friction that kills consistency. Treat it like a recurring client appointment that can't be moved.

• **Define the output upfront, not on shoot day** — Before Jordan arrives, give her a shot list: 60-second transformation testimonial with [specific member], 30-second class highlight reel, 3-5 social clips from peak energy class times. She knows exactly what to capture; no wasted time figuring it out on camera.

• **Schedule shoots during your busiest class times** — 6:30pm weekday classes = full gym, high energy, authentic footage. Boring empty gym footage kills engagement. Pick the 90-minute window when your studio looks most alive.

• **Assign one person (you or staff) as "Jordan's handler"** — They brief her on the day's focus, introduce her to the testimonial member beforehand, manage class flow so she's not disruptive. One point of contact = faster shoots, better content.

• **Batch-repurpose immediately** — Within 48 hours, you have a bank of clips. Don't wait. Post 2-3 pieces that week across Instagram/TikTok/Facebook. Momentum compounds; sporadic posting kills reach.

What's your busiest class time currently?

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Automating 6-Week Trial Enquiries for The Forge

• **Map the current manual bottleneck first** — Have Grace (or whoever handles trials) document exactly what happens NOW from enquiry to trial start: emails sent, follow-ups, payment collection, onboarding docs. This takes 30 mins but reveals where automation actually saves time vs. looks good on paper.

• **Set up a Zapier/Make workflow that auto-triggers on form submission** — Route enquiry → auto-email with trial welcome pack + booking link (Calendly) + payment link (Stripe) → auto-tag in CRM → reminder email 2 days before session. This cuts manual back-and-forth from 4-5 touches to 1.

• **Create ONE dead-simple booking/payment step** — Don't make it "enquire → email sequence → call → payment → booking." Make it enquire → instant Calendly + Stripe link in the first auto-response. Fewer steps = higher conversion on trials (which feeds membership sign-ups).

• **Pre-load the automation with The Forge's trial specifics** — Emails should mention women-only environment + beginner-friendly sessions + specific class times (not generic copy). This filter-qualifies enquiries and reduces no-shows.

• **Set a 6-week review checkpoint** — Track conversion rate (enquiry → trial attendance → membership), no-show rate, and time saved. If it's not hitting 50%+ trial-to-membership conversion, the automation isn't the problem — the trial experience itself needs fixing.

What's your current trial-to-membership conversion rate?

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Role-Specific Action Checklists for The Forge

**For JoJo (assume front desk/ops):**
• **Daily checklist:** Member check-ins logged, class capacity verified by 8am, payment issues flagged. **KPI owner:** 95%+ daily member satisfaction (pulse survey at sign-out), zero missed class bookings due to system error
• **Weekly:** Cash reconciliation, member retention calls on lapsed 7-14 day users, class schedule accuracy check. **KPI:** <2% payment discrepancies, contact 100% of lapsed members

**For Eilis (assume fitness/programming):**
• **Daily checklist:** Class delivery logged, 1:1 form checks on 3-5 members, program progression notes. **KPI owner:** Member strength progression (measured monthly—avg +5% lift improvement), class attendance rate >75% week-on-week
• **Weekly:** New program rollout testing, coach feedback synthesis, injury prevention audit. **KPI:** Zero safety incidents, 85%+ class NPS score

**For Sarah (assume marketing/growth):**
• **Daily checklist:** Social content posting (3x), inbound lead logging, retention email send. **KPI owner:** Lead volume (baseline first—set realistic target), email open rate >30%
• **Weekly:** Conversion funnel audit (visits→trials→members), competitor intel, 1 partnership outreach. **KPI:** Trial-to-member conversion >40%, cost-per-acquisition trending down

**Execution move:** Print these as **laminated desk cards** this week. Have each person tick daily. Review Fridays in 15-min huddles. Kill tasks

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# How Grace Executes This Vision Session (Not Generic Workshop Stuff)

• **Pre-session: Send the SSO + 90-day plan 48 hours ahead.** Grace should email the single-sentence objective and specific 90-day targets (e.g., "Add 40 members, hit £X revenue, launch referral program") to staff before the meeting. This kills the "deer in headlights" moment and lets them process. The session isn't explaining—it's *clarifying and aligning*.

• **Flip the standard agenda: Lead with "Here's your lane" first, then why it matters.** Start by assigning each person's exact responsibility (e.g., "Sarah owns member retention touchpoints, Jamie owns class scheduling for capacity," etc.). Then show *how their lane feeds the 90-day target*. People engage when they see their role's direct impact, not when drowning in company vision.

• **Use a one-page visual: SSO at top, three 90-day goals in the middle, each person's lane listed below with 2-3 KPIs they own.** Post it. Reference it constantly. Staff should leave knowing the three numbers they're chasing and whether they're hitting them weekly.

• **Close with accountability questions, not motivational speeches.** Ask each person: "What blockers do you hit this week doing your lane? What do you need from me?" This surfaces real friction *immediately* instead of discovering issues in week 5.

• **Schedule 15-min 1-on-1s with each team member within 2 days after.** Reinforce their lane, hear doubts privately, adjust if needed. The group session plants seeds; the 1-

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalance for The Forge (30-day execution)

**Week 1: Get the brutal truth**
- Have Grace spend 3 days shadowing each team member during their actual shifts (don't ask them to report it—watch it). Note: interruptions, admin tasks stealing time, bottlenecks where one person is the single point of failure.
- Use a simple Google Sheet: log every task each person does hourly. You'll spot patterns (e.g., front desk person doing all social media uploads at close of business = rush job, burned out).

**Week 2: Identify the specific pain points**
- One-on-one 20-min chats with each staff member—ask: "What takes the most time?" and "What would you hand off today if you could?" Listen for resentment (tells you who's drowning) and hidden skills (tells you rebalancing options).
- Flag tasks that *only* one person can do—these are your leverage points for delegation/training.

**Weeks 3-4: Rebalance with friction in mind**
- Don't just redistribute tasks. Pair overloaded person with someone underutilized for 2-3 shifts so knowledge transfers happen live, not via email.
- Give the newly-trained person one task fully, track for 2 weeks, then add the next. Gradual prevents resentment and errors.
- Make ONE person explicitly accountable for each task going forward—no ambiguity = no bottlenecks rebuilding.

**Measure it**: Same hourly log in week 4. If overtime hours dropped and task completion stayed consistent, you've won.

# Concrete Cost Cuts for The Forge (10% Target)

**1. Audit your membership software & utilities first** — These are usually quick wins. Call your gym software provider today and ask for a loyalty program discount or negotiate a lower per-member rate. Same with your energy supplier — most small businesses overpay. One call could save 2-3% alone.

**2. Negotiate supplier contracts with specifics** — Don't ask for "a better deal." Call your cleaning, laundry, and equipment maintenance providers with this: "I'm comparing three quotes for the next 12 months. What's your absolute best price?" Most will drop 5-10% to keep recurring revenue. Get written quotes before committing.

**3. Right-size your staffing hours** — Analyze your busiest hours by zone (classes vs. open floor). You're probably overstaffed during dead times. Shift 4-6 hours/week from quiet periods to peak hours. That's real money, no service hit.

**4. Renegotiate your lease or reduce square footage** — If you're 3+ years in, your landlord knows losing you is expensive. Ask about a 10% reduction in exchange for a 3-year renewal. Or genuinely explore smaller premises—sometimes downsizing actually improves member experience (tighter community feel).

**Quick math check:** Software/utilities (-2%), suppliers (-3%), staffing (-3%), lease (-2%) = 10%. Pick your best two and execute this week.

What's your current biggest expense category?

# Updating Monthly Finance KPIs at The Forge

**Lock in a 90-minute monthly block (same day every month)** — Thursday morning after you close books. Non-negotiable. Prevents it slipping and ensures data's fresh. Assign one person as "backup owner" so you're not the single point of failure.

**Pull only 7 KPIs max, not 20** — for a gym your size: (1) Monthly recurring revenue by membership tier, (2) Churn rate, (3) New member acquisition cost, (4) Class utilization %, (5) Personal training revenue, (6) Cash position, (7) Member lifetime value. Anything else is noise that distracts from decisions.

**Compare month-on-month AND vs. annual target in one visual** — one simple spreadsheet or dashboard tile. You need to spot trends (churn creeping up?) AND whether you're on track to hit annual goals. This is where growth decisions live.

**Share a 1-page summary with your team within 48 hours** — specific wins ("PT revenue +12%") and one thing you're fixing ("churn up 2%, running retention offer next week"). Transparency keeps everyone aligned on what matters and makes them notice patterns you might miss.

**Use it to trigger ONE decision monthly** — if CAC is climbing, pause paid ads and test referral incentives. If utilization dips, adjust class times. The KPIs only matter if they change what you *do* next.

What's your current reporting setup looking like?

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

**Build a 3-statement model in Excel (or use Stripe/accounting software templates) in the next 2 hours:**
- Pull last 12 months of actual revenue (membership fees, class packages, PT sessions) and segment by source
- List fixed costs (rent, staff salaries, insurance) and variable costs (cleaning supplies, equipment maintenance)
- Don't overthink — you need: monthly revenue projection for next 12 months, break-even membership count, and cash runway if revenue dips 20%

**Use your existing member data to stress-test assumptions:**
- What's your actual churn rate? Check: How many members joined vs. left last month
- If it's 5% monthly churn, factor that into membership forecasts — don't assume growth continues flat
- Cross-reference this against your current pipeline (free trials booked, referrals pending) to validate next quarter's intake targets

**Identify your ONE financial constraint right now:**
- Is it cash flow timing (revenue comes in weekly but rent's due monthly)? Or do you need X new memberships to hit breakeven? Or is equipment investment blocking growth?
- Your forecast should highlight this immediately — that's your growth bottleneck to solve next

**Make it a live document, not a static report:**
- Update it monthly with actuals vs. forecast — you'll spot revenue trends 4 weeks before they become problems
- Share the 3-5 key metrics with your team (breakeven point, monthly growth target) so everyone knows what "winning" looks like

**Action this week:** Do the model. You'll likely discover you're leaving 10-15% of potential revenue on the table somewhere

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Build Your Service P&L — Here's the Exact Move

**Track the three cost buckets that actually matter:**
- **Direct costs**: Instructor pay per class/PT session, music licensing, equipment replacement (depreciate monthly). For PT, include any commission/splits.
- **Allocated overhead**: Rent + utilities + insurance split proportionally (if classes run 10 hrs/week and gym is open 60 hrs/week, classes get ~17% of overhead). Add staff salary only where it's genuinely tied to that service.
- **Add-ons separately**: Supplements, merchandise, branded apparel — track COGS and actual margin separately from service delivery.

**Do this in a single spreadsheet — not separate P&Ls:**
- Revenue row: Monthly revenue per service (classes @ £X per member, PT @ £X per session, add-ons @ gross sales).
- Subtract direct costs. That's your contribution margin — the real number that matters.
- Allocated overhead on a line below so you see which services truly cover their share.
- **Quick win**: Pull 3 months of data this week. Don't wait for perfect data; actual messy numbers beat no numbers.

**The insight you're actually hunting for:**
You'll likely find PT has the fattest margin but lowest volume. Classes have thin margins but sticky members who buy add-ons. That changes your pricing and marketing strategy *immediately*.

**Action today**: List your 5 biggest service revenue sources and their instructor/delivery costs. That's your starting row.

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# The Forge: Automation Audit Execution

**1. Run a 2-hour manual trace NOW**
Pull your last 10 enquiries. Actually walk through each one: email received → system tag → follow-up trigger → trial booking. Document where they got stuck or duplicated. You'll spot the real breakdowns faster than reviewing automation logic in isolation.

**2. Kill the low-hanging duplicates immediately**
Bet money you have 2-3 automations firing the same "welcome" or "trial reminder" email. Search your automation dashboard for any rule that has the same trigger (e.g., "new lead from website form") firing to the same audience twice. Delete one copy today—don't overthink it.

**3. Test the critical path with a fake account**
Create a dummy Gmail address, submit an enquiry through your website, and see what you actually receive in what order. Check timing gaps (are reminders too bunched?), message quality, and whether the trial booking link is actually clickable. This takes 15 minutes and beats any theory.

**4. Fix the ONE biggest leak**
You probably have one step where women drop off (likely the trial booking confirmation or payment link). Once you identify it in step 1, prioritize fixing that instead of perfecting everything. One unbroken flow beats ten partially working ones.

**5. Document and assign**
Write a one-page SOP showing: trigger → action → expected delay → next step. Assign someone (you or staff) to check this weekly for 4 weeks—drift happens fast.

What's your biggest suspicion about where enquiries currently get lost?

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge — Concrete Action Plan

• **Task 1: Automate member onboarding emails** — Set up a Zapier workflow that triggers when a new member joins (via your booking system). Have it auto-send a welcome email, gym orientation guide, and class schedule. Then Slack yourself their name so you personally call within 24hrs. *Result: 3-4 hours/week saved, retention improves because they feel welcomed.*

• **Task 2: Build a ChatGPT-powered class inquiry responder** — Embed a simple chatbot on your website (or use a tool like Tidio) trained on your class times, pricing, and FAQs. It answers "What time is spin class?" or "Do you offer trials?" instantly. Captures leads automatically. *Result: You stop answering the same 10 questions daily — frees you to focus on sales calls.*

• **Task 3: Automate member re-engagement texts** — Use Twilio + Zapier to send a "We miss you" text to anyone who hasn't booked a class in 14 days, with a 1-click booking link. Personalize with their preferred class type. *Result: Catches churn before it happens; 5-10 min/week vs. manual follow-ups.*

**Start with #2 this week** — highest ROI. Takes 2 hours to set up, immediately stops wasting your time on repetitive DMs.

What's your biggest repetitive pain point right now?

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity for The Forge Website – Execution Plan

• **Get an SSL certificate TODAY** (not tomorrow). Use Let's Encrypt (free) or GoDaddy ($70/year). This takes 30 mins and shows "https://" — critical for member trust when they're entering payment info. No cert = members won't complete signups.

• **Fix your payment handling immediately.** If you're currently storing card details yourself, STOP. Switch to Stripe or Square (both handle PCI compliance for you). Grace, this single decision removes 90% of your legal/breach risk and is non-negotiable for a membership business.

• **Add a basic password policy to member accounts** — force 8+ characters, require password resets every 90 days. Implement via your website platform (Wix, WordPress, Squarespace all have built-in tools). Takes 1 hour, prevents member account takeovers.

• **Enable two-factor authentication on YOUR admin access** (Gmail, hosting account, payment dashboard). This is where attackers actually get in — not the website itself. Takes 15 mins, blocks 99% of account breaches.

• **Audit who has backend access right now.** Delete old staff logins, document current ones. One former employee with access = ransomware risk. Do this this week — it's free and takes 30 mins.

**Skip expensive consultants for now.** These four steps kill your biggest vulnerabilities. Revisit penetration testing once you're scaling to 500+ members.

marketing.advice:
# The Forge — Ad Performance Analysis & Recommendations

## Insights

**Your cost-per-lead has quadrupled (£6 → £27) whilst lead volume has collapsed 94% in four months.** This isn't a budget cut problem—it's a creative or targeting decay problem. You've gone from £435 spend generating 69 leads to £109 generating 4. The efficiency has tanked so badly that even your strong 75% close rate in July can't rescue the unit economics.

**Audience fatigue and creative staleness are the culprits.** April's performance suggests you had a working formula. By June, cost-per-lead had already tripled before July's implosion. Meta's algorithm is either showing your ads to increasingly poor-fit audiences (lookalike drift, interest overlap, frequency saturation) or your creative has stopped resonating. The fact that July's leads still converted well (75%) tells you the audience *quality* remains decent when you do reach them—you're just not reaching enough people who engage.

**You're massively underfunded relative to what the data says you should spend.** April proved you can profitably spend £435/month at £6/lead with a 29% close rate. July's £109 is survival mode, not growth. Your profit is propped up only by small sample size luck (3 sales). You need to restart with fresh creative and targeting *at scale* to rebuild lead volume.

---

## Actions

**1. Pause all current campaigns immediately and audit creative.** Pull your top 3 ad creatives from April. What was the hook? Single image vs. carousel? Copy tone? Testimonial-led? Pain-point led? Rebuild 4–5 entirely new creatives for August—test different angles: "6 weeks to feel strong" vs. "no scales, no ego" vs. before/afters from real members vs. a quick win (mobility/first session vibe). Launch these as fresh ad sets, not refreshes of old ones.

**2. Segment your audience ruthlessly.** Your April audiences were likely tighter. Test separately: (a) cold traffic aged 25–40 interested in fitness/wellness (broad but fresh); (b) lookalike audience built
